soap:
REVIEW · lance-format/lance#7497 — refactor(arrow): simplify normalize_slicing to a no-op
LuciferYang (core) · M (+18/-47, 1 file)

ONE-LINER
  Removes ~30 lines of `normalized_struct_array_data` (the offset-folding branch)
  from `StructArrayExt::normalize_slicing` and collapses the method to
  `Ok(self.clone())` guarded by a `debug_assert!`, on the claim that an arrow-rs
  `StructArray` can never carry a parent-level slice offset, so the branch was
  dead. Public API unchanged; docs rewritten to state the invariant.

FINDINGS  (each tied to ground truth)
  • rust/lance-arrow/src/struct.rs:50-63 — new body is `debug_assert!(offset==0 &&
    all columns.len()==len)` then `Ok(self.clone())`. Logically equivalent to the
    OLD early-return guard (old line: `if self.offset()==0 && columns all len==len
    { return Ok(self.clone()) }`). The only behavioral difference is what happens
    when that predicate is FALSE: old code ran `normalized_struct_array_data`; new
    code returns `self.clone()` unchanged (and panics only in debug builds).
  • Central "dead branch" claim — VERIFIED against the test the author cites.
    test_arrow_cpp_slicing (struct.rs:143-161) builds raw `ArrayData` with
    `.offset(1).len(2)` then `make_array(...).as_struct()`. This is exactly the
    C Data Interface import shape (`arrow::ffi::from_ffi` lands in `make_array`),
    and the test asserts the result equals `struct_array.slice(1,2)` — i.e. the
    offset was already pushed into children at construction. So the FFI path the
    author flags as the one residual risk is in fact already covered: arrow-rs
    normalizes at `make_array`/`StructArray::from(ArrayData)` time.
  • Covered by tests: test_normalize_slicing_no_offset (no-offset), test_arrow_rs_slicing
    (Array::slice path), test_arrow_cpp_slicing (raw ArrayData/FFI path). All three
    exercise the early-return predicate and are unmodified — so they pass iff the
    predicate held for every shape tested, which is the author's whole claim. PR
    states cargo test/clippy/fmt green; packet merge_state.ci = green.
  • Callers checked (the write-path consequence). Two external callers, both on the
    encode/write path:
      - rust/lance-encoding/src/encodings/logical/struct.rs:431 (StructStructuralEncoder::maybe_encode)
      - rust/lance/src/dataset/blob.rs:642 (preprocess_struct_array)
    Both take a public `ArrayRef`, downcast to StructArray, call normalize_slicing,
    then pushdown_nulls + per-child processing. If a non-normalized StructArray ever
    reached either, the OLD code corrected it; the NEW code in a RELEASE build
    silently encodes the un-normalized array (debug_assert compiled out). No such
    path exists today, but this is the one place the change is not behavior-preserving.
  • arrow_data import removal (struct.rs:8) — `ArrayData`/`ArrayDataBuilder` were
    only used by the deleted fn; pushdown_nulls uses `to_data()`/`make_array` which
    come from arrow_array. Import removal is correct, no other use in file.

ASSESSMENT
  risk: low · urgency: high — Refining the path-only baseline (med) DOWN to low,
    with evidence: this is a pure deletion of a provably-dead branch in a small
    leaf crate; the retained guard is logically identical to the prior early-return;
    all three pre-existing tests (including the FFI-shaped one) exercise the claim
    and are unmodified. The only non-equivalence is release-build behavior on an
    input that cannot currently be produced. Not an IO-correctness or wire-compat
    break in any reachable path.
  escalation: none (escalation.forced = false)
  could not read: none (0 omitted files)

DRAFT COMMENTS
  praise: Nice tightening — the rewritten trait doc now states the real arrow-rs
    invariant instead of the old "both are legal (╥﹏╥)" hand-wave, and the deleted
    branch was genuinely dead (test_arrow_cpp_slicing already proves make_array
    normalizes the FFI shape).

  suggestion (non-blocking): Release-build safety net. `debug_assert!` is compiled
    out in release, so if a future arrow-rs change (or an FFI path we haven't
    foreseen) ever yields a non-normalized StructArray, the two encode-path callers
    (struct.rs:431, blob.rs:642) would silently encode it instead of correcting it.
    Consider either (a) returning `ArrowError::InvalidArgumentError` when the
    invariant fails so it surfaces in release too, or (b) keeping a cheap
    always-on check. You already offered to retain the original normalization as a
    defensive net — that also works. Author's call; current behavior is unchanged.

RECOMMENDATION: approve
  confidence: high · what would make this wrong: an arrow-rs version (or an FFI /
    C Data Interface import path) that constructs a StructArray with a non-zero
    parent offset and children NOT pre-sliced. The cited test and make_array
    semantics indicate this is not representable today; if a reviewer knows such a
    path the suggestion above (return Err vs debug_assert) becomes the fix, not a
    revert.
